svg.title: Covariance and Correlation

div.style: position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
div.p: Covariance and correlation are two fundamental concepts in statistics used to describe the relationship between two random variables.

div.style: position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
div.p: Covariance measures the linear relationship between two random variables X and Y.

div.style: position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
div.p: Correlation is a standardized measure of covariance, ranging from -1 to 1.
Correlation coefficient (ρ) is calculated as: ρ = Cov(X, Y) / (σX \* σY)

div.style: position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
div.p: The mathematical definition of covariance and correlation can be derived from the following assumptions:
X and Y are random variables.
The joint probability distribution of X and Y is known.
Cov(X, Y) = E[(X - E(X))(Y - E(Y))] = E[

div.style: position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
div.p: • Positive covariance: X and Y tend to increase together.
• Negative covariance: X and Y tend to decrease together.
• Zero covariance: X and Y are unrelated.

div.style: position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
div.p: Covariance and correlation have numerous real-world applications, including:
• Finance: forecasting stock prices and portfolio optimization.
• Healthcare: analyzing the relationship between disease rates and environmental factors.
• Social sciences: studying the correlation between variables in social phenomena.

div.style: position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
div.p: Suppose we want to analyze the relationship between the number of hours studied by students and their exam scores.
Covariance and correlation can help us determine if there is a linear relationship between these variables.

svg.footer: Learn more about Covariance and Correlation
